author: @DanA-nl5uo
text: Robert part of what you are seeing is there are actually incentives in china to hire people and not replace them with autonation.  I was told by a large Chinese company that they didn't want me to automate several steps in their process because they needed to employ a certain number of people in the factory and the process I had a design solution for would have put them below their quota.

author: @Gwydion67
text:
Considering many of the comments yet given on the surpisingly high part of manual work, I'd say: Yes, due to the lower income of the employees this would be reality even in bigger Chinese factories, but a lot less ln "western" ones.
But don't get it wrong: There are other asian countries with even lower working standards (not 100% sure of the names, so I better leave it this way, you may look it up yourself, as I will do later).

What I also would like to add:
Fascinating, how simple the basic steps are. Which surely is what Robert was going to say in his video.
And what should empower anyone to play around with the stuff. At least I do for the last two years, like I've described a bit as comment of a comment below, a couple of minutes ago.

Robert, in the end your own developed battery will be literally a blast (may they stay intact and you staying healthy in reality)! 🤩🤞

author: @ecospider5
text:
And this is why tesla uses round cells. The sheet is made. It goes into a roll like at the beginning of this process but smaller. And they stuff it into a round case. 

Obviously there are more steps and a couple more sheets go into the roll, separator and such. But it is fully automated with no hands needed. 

Not useful for thin phones though.

author: @spacecase0
text:
I bought some batteries from some company in China. Shipping was just as much as the batteries cost. They claimed their battery process was way more reliable than other companies (LiFePO4). They only showed pictures of machines and not any workers.
Thank you for demystifying battery making. Pretty sure I'm going to try making my own rather than sending money to other countries looking for batteries that will work.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: awesome mate

author: @edwardcdg
text:
Argentina and Bolivia are said to have large lithium deposits.  Both have been trying (sort of) to get someone to invest in making lithium mines.  Unsuccessfully.  I think they are kleptocracies, unable to accumulate capital honestly and unable to convince anyone else to commit $$$.    They should have been trying to get into the lithium battery business with much higher margins than mining.  
This video tells that it ain't that hard.
(I was slow to respond to this.  I have been watching my country escape from insanity.)

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: it really isn't that hard mate - a little demystification is all that is needed really

author: @edkoetsier339
text: The manual process in those clips makes the Elon Musk totally automated process (from raw materials to finished pack ready to be installed in a car) look like magic...... I suppose in china they have so many people willing to act like drones, it's more cost effective to do it the age old way....

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: to be honest mate it frequently is!

replies.author: @edkoetsier339
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering But in the end, automation will win out. And the Chinese will steal all Elons ideas.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: @edkoetsier339 I think it depends mate - to my mind it's really a cost issue not a QC issue - when the cost of labour goes above the cost of automation - well automation will win - if the reverse is true manual labour wins - I think that's why manufacture is growing rapidly in countries with historically low wage costs

replies.author: @edkoetsier339
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering Manual battery manufacturing is a dirty and dangerous business. One of those industries where the human race as a whole benefits from automation. It's hardly an artisan endeavour. Anyway, in 20 years there won't be any assembly line factories anywhere. In 50 years, human jobs will be hard to come by. I hope we go the way of Star Trek , and not the way of Blade Runner. And by the by, wages are going up slowly in China. The people demanded it, quietly, and respectfully.

replies.author: @williamarmstrong7199
replies.text: Fine for development work.. but serious production?? That whole place is a quality control nightmare. The quality of the cells produced there and their longevity will vary dramatically based on local humidity levels. This is not a problem in a lab where in reality its all proof of principle. When its scaled to manufacturing levels.. its entirely different if you want any consistency.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: cheers mate

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I am sorry to contradict you mate but I think you wrong in part of what you say - you are right consistency and QC will be compromised but only to an extent and what I showed is serious production so it is clearly fine for serious production.

author: @comrademessenger
text: Id love to know your opinion on re-industrializing England,  can machines do away with hand labour? I do manual labor and much prefer it to sitting at a desk or being a cuck, but it’s the volume of needs as see being a problem. Do you scientific/industrious types talk about this? I’d love to know

replies.author: @hootsmin
replies.text: Once the carbon taxes kick in and the cost of moving goods to market from distant shores becomes more expensive your going to see some of the manufacturing coming back to local shores and somewhat of a pullback of the globalisation process.  The industries of the future are not going to look like the industries of the past though, most of the work is going to be done by machines or biotechnology.  Theres going to be lots of 3d printing and machining, probably a heavy use of genetically engineered microbes producing all sorts of chemicals and manufacturing materials.  There probably wont be the same amount of jobs as before and they will require high tech skills.  Theres a lot of information about the 4th industrial revolution on YT, have a search around.  Your seeing the destruction of the old system just now to bring this new system in, i.e. the revolution has allready begun.

author: @MaleAdaptor
text: Can lithium batteries be refurbished? I have been saving them from my old computers etc for years.

replies.author: @mikebond6328
replies.text: That is a very good question.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: not really mate

replies.author: @Gwydion67
replies.text:
Reclaimed / reused, I would say "yes", but only after thorough testing and sorting them into classes of similar capacity. At least, so do I with old notebook batteries I got from my company's IT department.
And those too far from any other will serve applications with use if a single cell only.

Total recycling on the other hand requires a vast amount of heat, plus severe protection from poisenous fumes / gases (among other critical parameters).
I personally haven't opened up a single cell in years.

But to face it: My average dropout is less than 5%.
And the benefit of reused Li-batteries is simply awsome: Powerbanks, garden and indoor lamps, even replacing old NiCd batteries from an old cordless drill with newer but also used notebook batteries (I cannot wait to watch Robert's next video to the same topic! 😃).
The applications are endless!
@Robert: Don't you agree?

author: @HeatherNaturaly
text:
DO you know what the difference is between a single use battery and a rechargeable one? 
I make a habit of attempting to recharge all my single use batteries. Some work a couple of times before they separate and fizz.

replies.author: @charlesdickens6706
replies.text: .....the key to recharge of non-rechargeable  batteries is to not recharge them    but rather top them up and never letting them deplete to less than say 65% . Then there's how the charge current delivery is configured as doses to minimise dendrites .

replies.author: @HeatherNaturaly
replies.text: @charlesdickens6706 I have NO IDEA what you are talking about. "Then there's how the charge current delivery is configured as doses to minimise dendrites ." Huh?????????

replies.author: @getyourbone
replies.text: @HeatherNaturaly I believe… But don’t know… Means charge in pulses… On off on off on off…?🤷🏻‍♂️

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: yes - but it is a rather long winded description - I may well do a video on it - cheers

replies.author: @charlesdickens6706
replies.text: @HeatherNaturaly ....zinc has strong tendency to form dendrites,, they're long thin needle like spikes ,,crystals in fact,, that pierce through separator and cause a short or worse if it happens for  lithium batteries ,, lithium may also form dendrites and lead also can short out from dendrites come to think of it. Anyway the non-rechargeable batteries are carbon zinc and a long time ago it was found that to diminish dendrites forming during the charging (topping up )  process that a modulated charge current gave better results , it was basically two steps forward and one step backwards ; for example you'd use the alternating current to give say 200 milliamps of  charge on one cycle of a hundredth of a second by means of diode and resistor followed by a discharge of nominally 100milliamps for the next hundredth of a second by use of reversed diode and doubled resistance.. This was said also to be a better charge process for rechargeable NiCds also but not an efficient way to charge  clearly. .There were chargers on the market once too for doing top up charging of non-rechargeable batteries . . . .and so Rob may do a video , good material for a video .
